title: The Six Swans
summary: A silent princess endures hardship to save her brothers from a wicked enchantment.
full_text:
A king once hunted deep in a vast forest, pursuing a wild beast with such eagerness that he left his attendants far behind. When evening fell, he realized he was lost. Wandering through the deepening gloom, he encountered an old woman with a head that nodded perpetually—a witch. She promised to show him the way out, but on one condition: he must marry her daughter.

Desperate, the king agreed. The witch led him to her cottage, where her daughter sat by the fire. She was beautiful, yet the king could not look at her without shuddering. Nevertheless, bound by his promise, he married her and brought her to his palace.

But the king had been married before, and his first wife had born him seven children—six princes and one princess. Fearing his new wife would harm them, he hid them in a secret castle deep in the forest, visiting them with a magical clew of yarn that showed the way. The wicked stepmother, suspecting his absences, bribed the servants and discovered the truth.

The queen, versed in witchcraft from her mother, sewed six enchanted silk shirts and set out for the forest. When the children saw someone approaching, they ran joyfully to meet what they thought was their father. The stepmother threw the shirts over the six princes, and at once they were transformed into swans, flying away through the trees. Only the princess, who had not run out, remained.

When the king arrived and found only his daughter, his grief was profound. The princess, terrified of her stepmother, fled into the forest that night. She walked until she collapsed before a small hut. Inside, she found six little beds. As the sun set, six swans flew in at the window. As they landed, they blew upon one another until their swan skins fell away—and there stood her brothers.

"Free us," they said, "if you can. You must not speak or laugh for six years, and you must sew six shirts from starwort." Weeping, she promised.

She climbed a tree in the deep forest and began to weave. Days became months, months became years. She was discovered by the king of that country, who fell in love with her silence and her beauty. He married her despite his mother's objections.

But the king's mother hated the silent queen. When the queen gave birth, the mother-in-law stole each child and accused the queen of eating them. Three times this happened. At last, the silent queen was condemned to burn at the stake.

The day of execution was the last day of the six years. She had finished five shirts. The sixth lacked only its left sleeve. As she stood at the stake and the flames began to rise, six swans appeared in the sky. She threw the shirts over them, and at once the swan skins fell away, revealing six princes—the youngest with a swan's wing instead of a left arm. The queen could finally speak. She told the king everything. The wicked mother-in-law was burned in her place.

The king and queen and the six brothers lived in peace for many years, and the youngest prince wore his swan's wing as a reminder of the sacrifice that had saved them all.
moral: True love and devotion require patience and sacrifice.

title: The Erlking
summary: A father rode through the dark forest late at night, his feverish child pressed close against his chest.
full_text:
A father rode through the dark forest late at night, his feverish child pressed close against his chest. The wind howled through the ancient trees, and mist rose from the damp ground like the breath of buried things. The horse's hooves beat a desperate rhythm against the forest path, for the father knew his son was sick—so sick that every moment brought them closer to the edge of something terrible.

"Father," whispered the boy, his voice thin and trembling, "do you see him? The Erlking? He has a crown of shadows and a long, trailing robe."

The father saw nothing but the swirling fog. "Be still, my son," he said gently. "It is only the mist playing tricks upon your eyes."

But the boy could not be comforted. "He speaks to me, Father. He promises me wonderful games and flowers that bloom even in winter. His daughters will dance for me."

The father held his son tighter and spurred the horse onward. In his heart, dread coiled like a serpent. He knew the old stories—knew of the Erlking, the alder king, the spirit of the dark woods who whispered to children in the voices of those they loved, luring them from their beds by sweet promises.

"Come with me, dear child," the Erlking crooned in a voice that sounded like wind through dried leaves. "My daughters wait for you. They wear crowns of moonlight and gowns of spider silk. We shall dance until the stars grow tired."

The boy cried out, clutching his father's arm. "Father! He touches me! His hand is cold!"

The father's blood turned to ice. "My son, it is only the brush of a willow branch against your cheek. Hold fast. Our home is near."

But the Erlking's voice grew harsh. "If you will not come willingly, child, then I shall take you by force!"

The boy screamed—a terrible, piercing scream that rent the night. The father dug his heels into the horse's flanks and the beast surged forward. He could feel his son's body convulsing in his arms, could hear the ragged gasps coming faster and faster.

At last they reached the courtyard. The father threw himself from the saddle, cradling his child, and ran toward the lighted windows. But as he crossed the threshold, he felt a stillness settle over the small body in his arms. The boy's eyes were closed. His chest was still.

The Erlking had claimed his prize. And in the dark forest, among the ancient alder trees, a low, whistling laughter echoed through the night.

The ballad of the Erlking has been told in German lands for centuries, most famously set to music by Franz Schubert, whose frantic piano and anguished voice capture the terror of that midnight ride. But the story is older than any song—rooted in the belief that the forests of Europe were alive with spirits who resented human intrusion. The Erlking was not merely a monster; he was the forest itself, claiming what it believed was its due. To this day, parents in the Rhineland warn children not to stray from the path after dark, not to answer voices that call from the trees. For the Erlking still waits in the deep woods, his voice soft as rustling leaves, his hand cold as midnight dew, patient as the roots of the ancient oaks that have stood for a thousand years.
moral: Not all beautiful voices bring good intentions.
source: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, Der Erlkönig

title: The Pied Piper of Hamelin
summary: In the year 1284, the German town of Hamelin suffered a plague unlike any other.
full_text:
In the year 1284, the German town of Hamelin suffered a plague unlike any other. The rats came in waves—great armies of them pouring through every crack and crevice, scurrying across floors, nesting in walls, devouring grain and cheese, gnawing through beams. No cat could keep pace, no trap could stem the tide. The burghers of Hamelin met in desperate council, offering a fortune to anyone who could deliver them.

On the feast of Saints John and Paul, a stranger appeared at the west gate. He was tall and lean, dressed in a coat of many colors—pied, they called it—and he carried a silver pipe at his belt. None knew whence he came.

"For one thousand guilders," he declared, "I will rid Hamelin of every rat."

The burghers agreed.

The piper stepped into the street and raised his pipe to his lips. The first note was high and piercing. The second was lower, insistent. The third—and from every house, every barn, every cellar, the rats came tumbling. Great rats, small rats, brown and black and gray, a living river of fur and whiskers and gnashing teeth. They poured through the cobbled streets, following the piper down to the banks of the Weser River, and into the water they plunged, every last one drowning in the cold, swift current.

The people of Hamelin cheered. The plague was over.

But when the piper returned to collect his payment, the burghers offered him only fifty guilders. "Surely," they said, "a sorcerer such as yourself does this for love of the art." The piper's face darkened. He warned them of the danger of broken promises.

On the morning of June 26, while the adults were in church, the piper returned. He wore green like a hunter, and his face was terrible. He sounded his pipe once more—but this time the music was sweeter, stranger, filled with longing and distant laughter. The children of Hamelin heard it. They stopped their games and turned their heads. The music promised wonders—a land where streams flowed with honey, where fruit trees bent low with gold, where children never grew old. They followed the piper through the streets, one hundred and thirty of them, a silent procession.

The piper led them to the Koppenberg, a hill on the edge of town. There, a door opened in the mountainside. The piper entered, and the children followed. Only three were left behind—one lame, one deaf, one blind. The children were never seen again.

The street where the piper walked was forever after called Bungenstrasse, where no music may be played, where wedding processions silence their instruments in memory of that terrible day. The town chronicles recorded the event: "On the day of Saints John and Paul, 130 children departed, led by a piper, and were never seen again." The city hall itself bore an inscription documenting the tragedy for centuries.

But some say deep within the mountain, the children still dance—young forever—waiting for the piper's return. Others claim they emerged centuries later in Transylvania, founding a new settlement whose inhabitants all spoke a strange dialect of German. Scholars have debated the truth of the Pied Piper for generations. Was he a rat-catcher cheated of his fee? A child recruiter for Eastern European settlements? A pagan god reclaiming the children of a faithless town? No one knows. The mystery endures, and the story reminds us that promises, once broken, can never be fully mended.
moral: A promise broken brings terrible retribution.

title: The Iron Pig
summary: A kind boy treats a mysterious iron pig with tenderness and discovers a hidden treasure.
full_text:
In the heart of the Black Forest, where the pines grow so thick that the sunlight only reaches the ground in shivering slivers of gold, lived a woodcutter and his family. They were a people of the earth, their hands calloused and their faces weathered by the bite of winter and the humidity of the summer. Their home was a small, leaning cottage of timber and clay, and though they were poor in coin, they were rich in spirit—or so the woodcutter hoped. His two eldest sons were strong and bold, but they possessed a restlessness that bordered on arrogance. They viewed the forest as a resource to be conquered, the trees as obstacles to be felled, and the land as something to be bled for profit.

Then there was the youngest son, Elias. Elias was different. He was a quiet boy with eyes the color of autumn leaves and a heart that beat in time with the wind in the branches. While his brothers spent their days competing to see who could fell the largest oak, Elias spent his wandering the hidden glens, talking to the birds and listening to the whispered secrets of the ancient stones. He found beauty in the smallest of things: the iridescent wing of a dragonfly, the intricate patterns of frost on a windowpane, the way the moss felt like a velvet carpet beneath his bare feet.

One winter, a famine struck the valley. The frost of that year was deeper and more cruel than any in living memory. The crops had failed, the livestock had grown lean, and the woodcutter's axes were blunt against the frozen bark. Hunger became a constant companion, a hollow ache that lived in the stomach and clouded the mind. The eldest brothers grew irritable and cruel, blaming the land and the spirits of the forest for their plight. They spent their nights arguing over the last scraps of rye bread, their voices echoing through the thin walls of the cottage.

One afternoon, Elias ventured deeper into the forest than he had ever dared, searching for any edible root or forgotten berry. As he pushed through a thicket of frozen briars, he stumbled upon a hollowed-out ancient cedar, terms of its bark gnarled and twisted like a frozen scream. And there, nestled in the dark, damp center of the tree, he found it: a pig made entirely of iron.

It was a curious object, no larger than a common piglet, cast in a heavy, dark metal that seemed to absorb the light around it. Its eyes were two small, polished pebbles of obsidian, and its skin was cold—not the cold of winter, but a deeper, timeless chill that spoke of the earth's core. Elias reached out and touched the metal. He felt a strange vibration, a low hum that resonated in his very bones, as if the iron pig were breathing in its own slow, metallic way.

He carried the pig home, wrapped in his tattered cloak. When he showed it to his family, his brothers laughed. "A piece of junk!" the eldest roared. "You waste your time with scrap metal while we starve!"

But Elias felt a kinship with the object. He did not see a piece of scrap; he saw a creature that was alone and forgotten, much like himself in his own family. He placed the iron pig on the hearth, and every night, he shared his meager portion of porridge with it. He would whisper stories of the forest to the metal pig, telling it of the singing streams and the dancing leaves, and he would polish its cold skin with a piece of old wool, treating it with a tenderness that his brothers found absurd.

Weeks passed, and the hunger grew worse. One night, as the fire died down to a few glowing embers, the iron pig spoke. Its voice was not a voice at all, but a series of clicks and metallic clinks that formed words in Elias's mind. "You have fed me with your kindness," the pig whispered, "and you have warmed me with your heart. In a world of greed, you chose to give when you had nothing. For this, I shall reveal the secret of the forest."

As Elias watched, the iron pig began to glow with a soft, amber light. It leapt from the hearth, its metallic hooves clattering on the stone floor, and led Elias out into the moonlit forest. They traveled past the familiar paths, deeper into the wild, until they reached a waterfall that flowed upward, its waters defying gravity to climb a cliff of obsidian. At the base of the falls, the iron pig stopped and began to dig. With a single, powerful thrust of its iron snout, it overturned a slab of rock, revealing a cavern filled with treasures beyond imagining: gold coins from forgotten empires, jewels that held the colors of a thousand sunsets, and ancient artifacts of power and wisdom.

But the treasure was not the greatest gift. As Elias gazed upon the wealth, the iron pig transformed. The metal melted away, replaced by skin and fur, and in its place stood a guardian spirit of the forest—a being of light and greenery, with eyes that held the wisdom of the ages. "The gold is for your family," the spirit said, "but the wisdom is for you. Remember that the smallest act of kindness is a key that can open the heaviest door."

Elias returned home and shared the wealth with his family. His brothers, humbled and ashamed, learned to respect the forest and the kindness of their brother. The woodcutter's cottage was rebuilt, and the family never hungered again. But Elias never forgot the iron pig. He spent the rest of his days as the protector of the Black Forest, ensuring that the balance between man and nature was preserved, and teaching every child he met that the most valuable treasures are those that cannot be bought with gold, but only earned with a generous heart.
moral: Kindness and empathy are the most powerful keys to unlocking hidden treasures.

title: Hansel and Gretel
summary: Children abandoned in the forest discover a witch's gingerbread house and must use their wits to survive.
full_text:
Deep in the Black Forest, where the pines grow so thick they block the sun and the wind whispers secrets of old and forgotten things, there lived a poor woodcutter with his second wife. He had two children from his first marriage—Hansel, the elder brother, and Gretel, his sweet little sister. The stepmother hated them, secretly wishing they would disappear into the shadows of the pines. She convinced the woodcutter that there was not enough food for four people. "If we do not leave the children in the forest," she whispered, her voice a serpent's hiss, "we shall all starve together."

One morning, the father led the children deep into the forest, further than ever before. "Wait here," he said, his voice trembling, "while I fetch wood. Stay by the fire, and we shall return for you." But Hansel, a boy of keen ears and a sharper mind, had overheard the plan. He had spent the night gathering white pebbles from the garden, dropping them behind him like shimmering breadcrumbs. As the parents disappeared into the trees, the children waited until the moon rose, and by the light of the silver orb, Hansel followed the pebbles back home. The father wept with joy, but the stepmother's heart remained a frozen stone.

Months later, the famine returned, and the stepmother insisted on a second attempt. This time, the father locked the door at night, and Hansel could not gather pebbles. In desperation, he used a piece of hardened bread, crumbling it as they walked. But the birds of the forest—the crows and the jays—descended in a black cloud, devouring every crumb. The children were truly lost, wandering for days through a landscape of twisting roots and oppressive silence, until they encountered a bird with feathers of pure gold, who led them to a clearing.\n\nIn the center of the clearing stood a house that defied all reason. Its walls were made of gingerbread, frosting dripped from its windows like frozen waterfalls, and it smelled of cinnamon, sugar, and warm cloves. Driven by a hunger that had become a physical pain, Hansel and Gretel fell upon the house, tearing away pieces of the walls and licking the frosting from the eaves. As they feasted, the door creaked open, and an old woman emerged. She was hunched, her eyes clouded with cataracts, her voice a sugary croon that hid a predatory hunger. "Oh, you poor, lost children!" she exclaimed. "Come inside, and I shall feed you until you are plump and happy."

The hospitality was a facade. The woman was a witch—a child-eater who fattened her victims on sweets before roasting them in her oven. She locked Hansel in a small iron cage in the woodshed and forced Gretel to work as a slave, cooking great pots of stew and scrubbing floors. Every morning, the witch peered through the bars at Hansel and cursed, "Bones, not fat!" For she was too old and foolish to see that Hansel was holding out a small, dry bone instead of his finger, tricking her into believing he was still lean.

At last, the witch declared that tomorrow would be the day of the feast. Gretel, as the witch bent to check the oven's heat, realized it was a trap. "I do not know how to enter," she said innocently. "Could you show me how it is done?" The greedy witch, eager to finish her task, leaned forward and thrust her head into the oven. In a flash of courage, Gretel pushed her in and slammed the iron door shut. The witch's screams echoed through the cottage, but the children did not linger. They gathered the witch's hoard of diamonds and pearls, filled their pockets, and followed the golden bird back through the forest. When they finally reached their home, they found their stepmother gone and their father waiting for them with open arms, their poverty ended by the treasures they had brought back from the dark heart of the woods.
moral: Cunning and courage overcome even the darkest evil.

title: The Twelve Dancing Princesses
summary: Twelve princesses secretly dance every night in an enchanted underground kingdom, their secret revealed by a clever soldier.
full_text:
In a kingdom of towering spires and gilded halls, there lived a king who adored his twelve daughters above all else. They were as different as the months of the year, yet each possessed a beauty that was whispered about in every corner of the realm. However, the king was plagued by a mystery that defied all logic. Every morning, the princesses would wake in their beds, exhausted and pale, and their satin slippers were worn through—torn and frayed at the toes as if they had danced for a thousand miles. "Where do you go at night?" the king would ask, his voice filled with concern and suspicion. "We only sleep, dear father," the princesses would answer in a perfect, rehearsed unison, their eyes wide and innocent. But the king knew they lied.

Driven to desperation, the king issued a proclamation: any man who could discover the secret of the princesses' nightly disappearances would win the hand of the daughter of his choice and half the kingdom. Princes from distant lands arrived, lords of great renown, and scholars of the most arcane mysteries. They all entered the princesses' chambers at night, the doors locked from the outside. But one by one, they failed. Each morning, the men would emerge from the rooms in a deep, enchanted slumber, oblivious to the passing of the night, while the princesses smiled secretively at their failure.

A weary soldier, returning from years of war with a heart heavy with longing for a quiet life, stopped at the palace gates. On the road, he encountered an old woman with eyes like polished obsidian and a smile that knew too much. "I can tell you the secret," she whispered, "but you must follow my rules. First, do not drink the wine they offer you. Second, wear this cloak of invisibility, and above all, walk as softly as a shadow."

That night, the soldier lay in the youngest princess's room. As midnight struck, he heard a faint clicking sound. The princesses rose from their beds, their faces transforming from innocent daughters to mischievous conspirators. They dressed in gowns of starlight and silk, and the youngest, noticing the soldier's stillness, whispered to her sisters, "He is fast asleep, just like the others."

They opened a hidden door in the wall, and a spiral staircase of moonlight led them down, deeper and deeper into the earth, until they reached an underground hall of breathtaking splendor. There, twelve princes waited for them—princes from a realm of dreams, their eyes glowing with an ethereal light. The music began—a symphony of harps and flutes that seemed to breathe with the heartbeat of the world. The princesses and princes danced, their movements a blur of grace and passion, spinning in circles that defied the laws of physics. The soldier watched, mesmerized, as the dancers moved through clouds of iridescent mist, their slippers wearing thin with every beat of the celestial music.

As dawn approached, the dancers began their ascent. But the soldier, having remained awake and invisible, snatched a twig from a silver tree as he left the hall, leaving a trace of his presence. When he returned to the king, he did not reveal the secret immediately. He waited until the following morning, when the princesses appeared with their worn-out shoes. He stepped forward and presented the silver twig, the traces of the underground world still clinging to its leaves. The king's eyes widened. "The underground hall!" he exclaimed. The princesses, seeing their secret laid bare, wept and confessed everything.

They were married to the princes of the dream realm, and the soldier, choosing the youngest princess for her kind heart and brave spirit, took his place as the heir to the kingdom. He spent the rest of his days in peace, though sometimes, on the quietest nights, he could still hear the distant echo of the celestial music, reminding him that the world is far more mysterious than it appears.
moral: Truth cannot be hidden forever, and patience and observation are the keys to uncovering the deepest mysteries.

title: The Nix of the Millpond
summary: A miller's daughter must outsmart a malevolent water spirit to save her brother from a desperate bargain made by her father.
full_text:
There was once a poor miller who lived with his wife and daughter beside a dark, deep millpond in the hills of Upper Lusatia. The mill had been in his family for generations, but times had grown hard, and the miller struggled to feed his family. Every day he worked from dawn until dusk, but the mill brought little grain and less coin. One evening, as the miller stood at the edge of the pond staring into the black water and lamenting his misfortune, a strange figure rose from the depths. She was beautiful, with long green hair that flowed like seaweed and skin as pale as moonlight. Her eyes glowed with an otherworldly light. She was a Nix, a water spirit of the old German forests.

"Why do you weep, miller?" the Nix asked, her voice like the sound of water over stones. The miller, too desperate to be afraid, told her of his troubles. The Nix smiled, showing teeth as sharp as a pike's. "I can give you wealth beyond your dreams," she said. "Gold, silver, prosperity for your mill. But you must give me something in return." "Anything," the miller said recklessly. "Give me what was born in your house this morning," the Nix said. The miller thought for a moment. His old cat had given birth to a litter of kittens that very morning. Surely one kitten would be a small price to pay for endless riches. "Agreed," he said.

The Nix sank back into the pond, and from that day forward, the miller's fortunes changed. The mill wheels turned faster than ever before. The grain bins overflowed. Gold coins appeared in the flour sacks. The miller grew rich and prosperous. But when he returned home that evening, his wife met him at the door with tears streaming down her face. "Oh, husband," she cried, "our daughter gave birth to a son this morning!" The miller's blood ran cold. He realized what he had done. The Nix had tricked him. It was not a kitten, but his own grandson that he had promised to the water spirit.

Years passed, and the boy grew into a bright, spirited child. The Nix, however, never forgot her debt. Every year, on the anniversary of the bargain, the water of the millpond would turn a deep, bruised purple, and the Nix would appear, demanding the child. The miller, terrified, would offer her gold and jewels to delay the claim, and the Nix, amused by his fear, would agree for one more year. But the miller's daughter, a clever and brave girl, noticed the pattern. She knew that the Nix did not want gold—she wanted the soul of a child. When the boy turned seven, the Nix refused any more bribes. "The time has come," she hissed. "Bring me the boy, or I shall swallow your mill and your family whole."

As the miller wept in despair, his daughter stepped forward. "I will go to the pond," she declared. "I will make a new bargain for my brother." She went to the edge of the water and called to the Nix. "Spirit of the depths, I offer you a trade. I will give you a secret that no water spirit knows, a truth from the world of men, if you release my brother from your contract forever." The Nix, curious and arrogant, agreed. The daughter leaned in and whispered a secret: she told the Nix that true love is the only force that can make a human heart beat faster than fear. The Nix, who had known only hunger and cold in her depths, was momentarily stunned by the concept of love. In that moment of hesitation, the girl cast a handful of iron salt into the pond—the one thing a Nix cannot endure. The spirit shrieked and dove deep into the mud, the contract shattered by the iron's purity. The boy was safe, and the miller's family lived in peace, having learned that wealth gained through sacrifice is a burden, but love is the only true prosperity.
moral: Desperate bargains often carry a hidden cost; only courage and love can break the chains of greed and trickery.
source: German folk tradition - collected by the Brothers Grimm

title: The Frog Prince
summary: In the days when wishes still worked and magic was as common as morning dew, there lived a king whose youngest daughter was so beautiful that the sun itself stopped to stare when she stepped into the garden.
full_text:
In the days when wishes still worked and magic was as common as morning dew, there lived a king whose youngest daughter was so beautiful that the sun itself stopped to stare when she stepped into the garden. She was kind-hearted but willful, used to getting whatever she desired.

Near the king's castle lay a dark, ancient forest, and in that forest was a well so deep that no one had ever seen the bottom. On hot summer days, the princess would sit by this well and play with her favorite toy: a golden ball that sparkled like captured sunlight.

One afternoon, she tossed the ball higher than usual, and it slipped through her fingers. It bounced once, twice, and rolled into the well. The princess peered into the darkness and heard a faint splash as the ball disappeared into the black water. She burst into tears.

A voice rose from the water. "Why do you weep, princess?" It was a frog. His skin was green and bumpy, his eyes bulged, and his voice was a croak. The princess was startled. "My golden ball has fallen into the well," she said.

"I will retrieve it for you," said the frog, "if you promise to let me be your companion. Let me eat from your plate and sleep on your pillow." The princess agreed without thinking, desperate for her ball. The frog dove, retrieved the golden ball, and tossed it onto the grass. The princess grabbed it and ran home without looking back.

That night, a knocking came at the castle door. The king opened it to find the frog on the step. "Your daughter made a promise," the frog said. The king listened to the story and told his daughter, "A promise is a promise." Reluctantly, she let the frog eat from her plate.

But when the frog demanded to sleep on her pillow, the princess lost her temper. She grabbed the frog and threw him against the wall. Instead of a dying croak, there was a flash of light. The frog transformed into a handsome prince. He had been cursed by a wicked witch, and only a princess who kept her promise could break the spell.

They were married the next day. The princess learned that true beauty is not about appearance but about honor. The frog taught her that every creature deserves dignity, and a promise, once given, must never be broken.
moral: Do not judge by appearances.

title: Rapunzel
summary: Once upon a time, a married couple lived next to an old witch's garden.. The wife became pregnant and craved the rapunzel plants growing there.
full_text:
Once upon a time, a married couple lived next to an old witch's garden. The wife became pregnant and craved the rapunzel plants growing there. Her husband, unable to resist her pleas, stole some plants from the garden at night.

When the witch discovered the theft, she came to their home in great anger. You have stolen my plants,' she cried, and now you must give me your child!' The couple had no choice but to surrender their newborn daughter.

The witch took the baby and named her Rapunzel after the plants she had craved. Rapunzel grew into the most beautiful girl with hair as golden as the sun. The witch kept her locked in a tall tower hidden deep in the forest, with no door and no stairs—only a window at the very top.

When Rapunzel was twelve years old, a young prince was hunting in the forest and heard beautiful singing. He followed the voice and discovered the tower. He called up to her, and Rapunzel let down her long golden hair so he could climb up. They met every day, and soon they fell deeply in love.

One day, the witch discovered their secret meetings. In her rage, she cut off all of Rapunzels beautiful hair and banished her to the desert wasteland. Then she waited for the prince with the stolen hair.

When the prince came to visit, he called up to the window and the witch let down the hair. The prince climbed up and found only the wicked witch, who laughed at him. 'You will never see your beloved Rapunzel again! she screeched.

The prince was so heartbroken that he jumped from the tower. He did not die, but thorns on the ground poked his eyes and blinded him. Wandering in despair, he wandered for years through the wilderness, eating berries and roots to survive.

One day, by some miracle, he heard a familiar voice—a woman's voice. It was Rapunzel calling out to her twins, a boy and a girl she had given birth to in the desert. The prince ran toward the voice, and when he reached her, he wept with joy. His tears fell upon her face and into his eyes, and suddenly he could see again—he had not truly been blind.

The prince took Rapunzel and the twins to his kingdom, where they lived happily ever after. The couple who had raised him took care of the children as if they were their own grandchildren.

And so the power of true love overcame all obstacles, as it always does.
moral: Love finds its way.

title: Cinderella
summary: A mistreated girl goes to the ball with magic help. Centers on Fairy,  from Europe. nature-spirit associated with forest and meadow..
full_text:
A rich man's wife fell sick. On her deathbed, she called her daughter: Be good and pious, and God will protect you. I will watch over you from heaven.' She died. The girl visited her mothers grave daily, weeping.

When winter came, the father remarried—a cruel woman with two daughters. They were beautiful but had black hearts. 'Why should that stupid goose sit in the parlor? they mocked. They gave Cinderella old gray clothes and wooden shoes. 'Look at the proud princess! they laughed.

Cinderella worked from dawn to dusk—carrying water, making fires, cooking, washing. She slept by the hearth in ashes, covered in dust—so they called her Cinderella.

One day, the father went to the fair. The stepsisters asked for beautiful dresses and jewels. Cinderella, shy, asked for the first branch that hits his hat on the way home. He brought it—she planted it on her mother's grave. Her tears watered it—and it grew into a beautiful tree.

When she shook the tree, a white bird dropped whatever she wished for.

The king announced a three-day ball for the prince to choose a bride. The stepsisters were invited. Cinderella asked to go. You? In your dirty clothes? No!' But she went to the hazel tree: Shake, shake, little tree—silver and gold throw down to me!' A magnificent dress and golden slippers appeared.

At the ball, no one recognized her. The prince danced with no one else. At midnight, she fled—losing a slipper.

The prince announced: Whoever fits this glass slipper shall marry the prince.' The stepsisters tried—their toes were too fat, their heels too large. Then Cinderella appeared: Let me try.' The slipper fit perfectly.

She married the prince. Goodness triumphed over evil.

The lesson: Kindness and piety are rewarded.
moral: Good wins over evil.

title: The Wild Swans
summary: Far away, in a land where swallows fly when winter comes, there lived a king who had eleven sons and one daughter named Elisa.
full_text:
Far away, in a land where swallows fly when winter comes, there lived a king who had eleven sons and one daughter named Elisa. The eleven princes went to school with stars on their chests and swords at their sides, writing with diamond pens on golden slates. Elisa sat on a little stool of polished glass, turning the pages of a picture book worth half a kingdom. They were the happiest children in the world.
But the king married a wicked queen who had no love for her stepchildren. On the very day of the wedding, she gave them sand in a teacup and told them to pretend it was cake. A week later, she sent Elisa to live with peasants in the countryside. Her hatred for the princes burned even hotter. "Go out into the world and fend for yourselves!" she shrieked, throwing enchanted dust over them. "Fly like great birds without voices!" The eleven princes were transformed into eleven magnificent wild swans. With a strange, mournful cry, they rose from the courtyard and flew into the deep forest.
Elisa grew up in the peasant cottage, dreaming of her brothers. At fifteen, she returned to the palace, but the queen, seeing her beauty, rubbed her face with walnut juice and tangled her hair with foul ointments until even her father did not recognize her. Heartbroken, Elisa slipped away into the forest. She wandered for days, eating berries, drinking from streams, sleeping on moss.
One evening, she came to a lake. At sunset, eleven white swans with golden crowns descended from the sky. As the sun sank behind the mountains, the swans shed their feathers and became her brothers. They wept and embraced, telling of their enchantment. "When the sun rises, we become swans," said the eldest. "Only at sunset do we regain human form. We live across the sea, in a land even more beautiful than this."
"We will carry you there tomorrow," they said.
At dawn, the brothers became swans again. They lifted Elisa on their backs and flew across the vast ocean. The journey was perilous — storms raged, and the sun burned. Elisa prayed for strength.
On the far shore, she had a dream. A fairy appeared, resembling the old woman who had given her berries in the forest. "Your brothers can be saved," the fairy said, "but you must have courage. You must weave eleven shirts from stinging nettles that grow in churchyards and on graves. While you weave, you must not speak a single word — not one — until the shirts are finished. If you speak, your brothers will die."
Elisa set to work. She gathered nettles that blistered her hands. She crushed them with her bare feet and wove the fibers into thread. Night after night she worked, her hands covered in blisters. Her brothers wept to see her suffering, but she could not speak to comfort them.
One day, a great king hunting in the forest found Elisa and fell in love with her. He took her to his palace and married her, though she never spoke. The archbishop grew suspicious of her silence. "She is a witch," he whispered. "She practices dark magic."
Elisa continued weaving in the palace, even as her hands grew raw. The nettles ran out, and she had to gather more from the churchyard at night. The archbishop followed her and saw her among the graves. "She is a witch!" he declared.
Elisa was condemned to be burned at the stake. On the morning of her execution, she was taken through the streets in a cart, still weaving the last shirt. The people jeered. The executioner seized her arm — but at that moment, eleven swans descended from the sky, circling overhead.
Elisa threw the nettle shirts over the swans. In an instant, they became her eleven brothers — all except the youngest, who still had a swan's wing instead of an arm, for she had not finished the last sleeve.
"Now I may speak!" Elisa cried. "I am innocent!" She told the king everything. The archbishop was banished, and the brothers were restored to their kingdom. Elisa and her brothers returned home, where they lived together in peace, their bond unbroken by the years of silence.
moral: Love endures any sacrifice.

title: The Fisherman and His Wife
summary: There was once a poor fisherman who lived with his wife, Ilsebill, in a miserable hovel by the sea — a place so small and filthy it was scarcely better than a pigsty.
full_text:
There was once a poor fisherman who lived with his wife, Ilsebill, in a miserable hovel by the sea — a place so small and filthy it was scarcely better than a pigsty. Every day the fisherman cast his line into the sparkling water, hoping for a decent meal.
One day, his line jerked downward with tremendous force. He pulled and pulled until he dragged up a magnificent flounder. But this was no ordinary fish. It looked at him with intelligent eyes and spoke: "I pray you, let me live. I am not a real flounder but an enchanted prince. What good would killing me do? Put me back, I beg you."
The startled but kindhearted fisherman said, "A fish that can talk I shall certainly let go." He released the flounder, who swam away leaving a trail of blood behind him.
When the fisherman returned home and told his wife, she was furious. "You caught a magical fish that grants wishes, and you asked for nothing? Look at this stinking hovel! Go back and demand a proper cottage!"
The fisherman did not want to, but his wife gave him no peace. He returned to the shore, where the sea had turned from clear to green and yellow. He called out, "Flounder, flounder in the sea, come, I pray thee, here to me. For my wife, good Ilsebill, wills not as I'd have her will."
The flounder appeared. "What does she want?" "A cottage," the fisherman whispered. "Go home," said the flounder. "She has it already."
When the fisherman returned, the hovel was replaced by a charming cottage with a flower garden, a yard full of ducks and chickens, and a well-stocked pantry. Ilsebill was delighted — for a week. Then she said, "This cottage is too small. Go back and demand a stone castle."
The fisherman went, though his heart was heavy. The sea was purple with rage. "Go home," said the flounder. "She has her castle."
Ilsebill now lived in a grand castle with a hundred servants, gold and silver furniture, and a park half a mile long. But she was not satisfied. "I want to be king," she declared. The fisherman protested, but she screamed until he went back. The sea was dark gray and churning. "She wants to be king," the fisherman said. "She is king already," the flounder replied.
Ilsebill sat on a throne of gold and diamonds. But the next morning, she woke the fisherman. "I want to be emperor — emperor of all the lands!" The fisherman begged her to be content. She threatened him. The sea was black and boiling. "She wants to be emperor," the fisherman told the flounder. "Go home. She is emperor."
Ilsebill sat on a throne of solid gold, guarded by soldiers from a hundred nations. Still, she was not satisfied. "Husband," she said, "go back to the flounder. Tell him I want to be pope." The fisherman's blood ran cold. "Wife, how can you be pope? There is only one pope in all of Christendom." "I will be that pope!" she screamed.
The sea raged in a tempest. The fisherman could barely stand. "She wants to be pope." "Go home. She is pope."
Ilsebill sat enthroned in St. Peter's, wearing the triple crown. Kings knelt before her. But that night, she could not sleep. "Husband! Go back! Tell the flounder I want to be like God — to command the sun and moon to rise at my will!"
The fisherman crawled to the shore through a hurricane. "She wants to be like God," he cried.
The flounder looked at him with ancient, weary eyes. "Go home. You will find her back in the pigsty."
And there she was, sitting in their original hovel, the stinking, filthy shack by the sea, where they lived for the rest of their days.
moral: Contentment is wisdom.

title: The Goose Girl
summary: There once lived an old queen whose husband had died years before.. She had one beautiful daughter, who was promised in marriage to a prince in a distant kingdom.
full_text:
There once lived an old queen whose husband had died years before. She had one beautiful daughter, who was promised in marriage to a prince in a distant kingdom. When the time came for the princess to depart, the queen prepared a rich dowry — jewels, gold, fine garments, and two horses: a common nag for the waiting-maid, and for the princess, a magnificent horse named Falada, who could speak. Before leaving, the queen pricked her finger, let three drops of blood fall onto a handkerchief, and tucked it into her daughter's bodice. "Keep this carefully," she said. "It will protect you on the road."
The princess tucked the handkerchief into her bosom and set out. As they rode through the countryside, the princess grew thirsty. "Pray, fetch me water from the stream in my golden cup," she said to her maid.
"If you are thirsty," the maid replied rudely, "dismount yourself and drink. I will be your maid no longer." The gentle princess obeyed without complaint. As she bent over the water, the handkerchief fell from her bodice and floated away. The maid saw this and rejoiced, for she now had power over her mistress.
The maid forced the princess to exchange clothes. She mounted Falada and threatened to kill the princess unless she swore never to tell anyone what had happened. The poor princess had no choice.
When they arrived at the prince's palace, the maid was welcomed as the bride, while the true princess was given the lowliest task: tending geese in the fields with a boy named Conrad.
The false bride feared Falada, who could speak and reveal the truth. She ordered the horse killed. The true princess begged the knacker to nail Falada's head above the dark gateway through which she passed each morning with the geese. As she passed the gate, she would say, "Falada, Falada, hanging there," and the horse's head would answer, "Alas, young queen, if your mother knew, her heart would break in two."
One day, Conrad noticed the goose girl talking to the horse's head. He also saw that her hair gleamed like spun gold when the wind lifted her bonnet. He told the king, who became curious. The next day, the king hid behind the gateway and heard the conversation between the girl and the horse's head.
The king called the goose girl before him. She threw herself at his feet, weeping, and told him everything — but she had sworn an oath of silence about the betrayal. So she told him only that she had been forced to take a terrible oath. The king, wise and kind, said, "If you cannot tell me, then tell this iron stove." He left her alone in a room with an old stove, and she poured out her heart to the iron grating, believing no one could hear. But the king had hidden a pipe that carried her voice to him in the next room. He heard everything.
The king ordered a great feast. He seated the false bride on one side and the true princess on the other. He told the story of a maid who had betrayed her mistress. "What punishment does such a woman deserve?" he asked. The false bride, not recognizing the story, answered, "She should be stripped naked and thrown into a barrel lined with sharp nails, dragged through the streets by wild horses."
"You have pronounced your own sentence," said the king. The false bride was dealt with as she had described. The true princess married the prince, and Falada's head was buried in the garden, where a tree grew in his memory — a tree that whispered the truth to anyone who would listen.
moral: Truth always revealed.

title: The Six Servants
summary: A prince travels with six extraordinary servants who each possess unique abilities (super hearing, super sight, super strength, etc.). Together they overcome impossible challenges to win a princess.
full_text:
Long ago, an aged queen who was a sorceress ruled over a kingdom with her daughter, the most beautiful maiden beneath the sun. But the old woman's heart was black. Whenever a suitor appeared, she set him three impossible tasks, and when he failed, his head was struck off. Many princes had died this way.
A certain king's son heard of the maiden's beauty and declared, "I will go and win her." His father forbade it, but the prince fell so ill with longing that for seven years he lay sick, and no physician could cure him. "Go, then," the father relented. The prince rose from his bed, instantly well, and joyfully set out.
As he rode across a heath, he saw what looked like a great heap of hay. Drawing closer, he realized it was the stomach of a man, enormous as a mountain. "If you need a servant," the fat giant rumbled, "take me." The prince asked his talents. "I can stretch myself to become even vaster, and I can eat and drink more than a thousand men."
Next, he met a man lying on the ground with his ear pressed to the earth. "I can hear the grass growing," the Listener said. "And I just heard someone being beheaded in the queen's castle far away."
Then came a man so tall his legs could stride over mountains. "I can see across the whole world," the Looker said. "At this moment I see a man in Cathay counting his coins."
They found a man with a cloth tied over his eyes. "Why do you cover them?" the prince asked. "Because my gaze shatters everything I see," said the Sharp-Eyed Man. "If I remove this cloth, I can reduce mountains to gravel."
Further on, a man sat shivering in the midday sun. "I feel cold," he said, "even though you burn." Yet when they passed a freezing glacier, steam rose from his body. The Hot-and-Cold Man could endure any temperature.
The sixth servant was a man whose neck stretched like a serpent's. He could see over the highest walls and reach across chasms.
The prince took all six into his service. They arrived at the sorceress's castle. "I wish to marry your daughter," the prince announced. "By all means," the queen smiled wickedly. "But first, fetch my ring from the bottom of the Red Sea."
The Listener heard where the ring lay, wedged between two rocks. The Tall Man strode to the shore. The Stout Man drank the entire sea in seven gulps, leaving the seabed dry. The Sharp-Eyed Man spotted the ring, and the Long-Necked Man reached down and plucked it up.
The queen was enraged but smiled. "Well done. Now, for the second task — eat three hundred oxen, hair, hide, bones, and all, and drink three hundred casks of wine to the last drop."
The Stout Man stepped forward. "Leave this to me." He devoured every ox, crunched every bone, drank every cask, and patted his stomach. "I could have managed more."
The queen's face darkened. "For the third task, you must guard my daughter through the night without sleep. If she is gone by morning, you die."
The prince sat with the princess, but she drugged his wine and slipped away. The Listener, however, heard her footsteps through twenty locked doors. The Long-Necked Man saw her hiding in a distant cave. The Tall Man brought her back before dawn. The prince had passed all three tasks.
The furious queen tried to burn the prince at the stake, but the Hot-and-Cold Man sat in the fire and shivered. When she sent her army to kill them, the Sharp-Eyed Man removed his blindfold and shattered the soldiers to rubble.
The prince married the princess, and the six servants, their work done, departed to seek their own fortunes. The prince never forgot that his greatest strength lay not in himself, but in the unique gifts of those he had chosen to stand beside him.
moral: Every person has unique talents - together, diverse abilities can overcome any obstacle.

title: Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs
summary: A beautiful princess escapes her jealous stepmother-queen and finds refuge with seven dwarfs. The queen tricks her with a poisoned apple, but a prince's kiss awakens her.
full_text:
In the middle of winter, when snowflakes fell like feathers from heaven, a queen sat sewing at a window framed in black ebony. She pricked her finger, and three drops of blood fell onto the snow. "How I wish for a daughter," she sighed, "with skin as white as snow, lips as red as blood, and hair as black as ebony." Soon after, she gave birth to a baby girl who was exactly that — skin white as snow, lips red as blood, hair black as ebony. They named her Snow White. But the queen died in childbirth.
A year later, the king married again. His new wife was beautiful but vain and cruel. She possessed a magic mirror, and each morning she asked, "Mirror, mirror, on the wall, who is the fairest of them all?" The mirror always answered, "You, O Queen, are the fairest of all." This satisfied her, for she could not bear anyone being more beautiful than herself.
Snow White grew more beautiful each year. When she was seven, the mirror told the queen, "O Queen, you are fair, it is true, but Snow White is a thousand times fairer than you." The queen flew into a rage. From that moment, she hated Snow White with a jealousy that consumed her.
She called a huntsman. "Take Snow White into the forest and kill her. Bring me her heart as proof." The huntsman led Snow White into the woods, but when he drew his knife, the girl's innocent tears melted his heart. "Run away, poor child," he said. "Run deep into the forest and never return." He killed a wild boar and brought its heart to the queen, who believed it was Snow White's.
Alone in the vast forest, Snow White ran through brambles that tore her dress and past wild animals that somehow did not harm her. As night fell, she stumbled upon a tiny cottage, neat and orderly. She pushed open the door. Inside was a table set with seven plates, seven cups, seven knives, and seven spoons. On the walls hung seven little beds, each with a white coverlet. She was so hungry and tired that she ate a few bites from each plate and fell asleep across several of the beds.
When the seven dwarfs returned from their work mining gold in the mountains, they found a beautiful girl sleeping in their home. They were astonished by her loveliness and moved by her story. "You may stay with us," they said, "if you keep house — cook, wash, sew, and make the beds."
Snow White agreed, and the dwarfs warned her: "Beware the queen. She will try to find you. Let no one into the cottage while we are away."
The queen, consulting her mirror, learned that Snow White still lived. She disguised herself as an old peddler woman and traveled to the cottage. "Pretty laces for sale!" she called. Snow White, seeing only a harmless old woman, bought a pair of laces. The queen laced them so tightly that Snow White collapsed. The dwarfs returned just in time to cut the laces and revive her.
The queen tried again, this time with a poisoned comb. Snow White fell as if dead when the comb touched her hair, but the dwarfs removed it and saved her.
The third time, the queen prepared a poisoned apple — half red, half white. The white half was harmless; the red half was death. She disguised herself as a farmer's wife and approached the cottage. The dwarfs had warned Snow White to let no one in, but the queen held up the apple. "Taste this beautiful apple," she said, and bit the white half herself. Snow White, seeing the woman eat it safely, took a bite of the red half — and fell dead.
This time, the dwarfs could not revive her. She looked so beautiful, with rosy cheeks and peaceful expression, that they could not bear to bury her. Instead, they made a coffin of glass and gold and placed her on a hillside, taking turns watching over her.
Years passed. A prince traveling through the forest saw Snow White in her glass coffin and fell in love with her. "Let me have her," he begged the dwarfs. "I cannot live without her." The dwarfs, moved by his grief, agreed. As the prince's servants carried the coffin, they stumbled, and the piece of poisoned apple lodged in Snow White's throat dislodged. She woke.
The prince married Snow White that very day. The queen, invited to the wedding, was forced to put on red-hot iron shoes and dance until she dropped dead — a punishment for her cruelty. Snow White and the prince lived happily ever after, and the seven dwarfs were frequent guests at the royal palace, their friendship cherished forever. egg into the dark water. The salmon Boots had saved dove deep and brought the egg back in its mouth.

"Squeeze it," said the wolf. Boots squeezed, and far away, the giant screamed in agony. "Squeeze again," said the wolf, and when Boots did, the giant's voice came thin and desperate: "Spare me! I will restore your brothers and their brides!" Boots squeezed one last time. The egg shattered. The giant crumbled to dust.

The stone figures on the path stirred. The six princes and six princesses blinked, alive once more. Boots returned to the castle and claimed his bride — the most beautiful princess of them all. They rode home together, and when the old king saw his seven sons each with a bride, his heart overflowed. "But the loveliest of them all," he declared, "is the bride of my youngest." And Boots, who had been called a cinder-lad, sat at the head of the table, his princess at his side, and ruled the kingdom with wisdom and kindness for the rest of his days.
moral: Even the strongest enemy has a hidden weakness.

title: Briar Rose (Sleeping Beauty)
summary: In times long past, a king and queen ruled a prosperous kingdom. Centers on Fairy,  from Europe. nature-spirit associated with forest and meadow..
full_text:
In times long past, a king and queen ruled a prosperous kingdom. For years they had longed for a child. One day, as the queen bathed in a cool spring, a frog crawled out and spoke: "Your wish shall be fulfilled. Before the year ends, you shall bear a daughter." And so it happened. The queen gave birth to a daughter so beautiful that the king could not contain his joy.
To celebrate, the king ordained a great feast. He invited the twelve Wise Women of his kingdom to bestow their gifts upon the princess. But there were thirteen Wise Women, and the king had only twelve golden plates. One was left uninvited.
The feast was celebrated with splendor. As it ended, the twelve Wise Women presented their gifts one by one: virtue, beauty, riches, grace, song, dance — everything good in the world was bestowed upon the infant princess. But just as the eleventh had spoken, the uninvited thirteenth burst into the hall. Her eyes burned with fury. Without greeting, she pointed a bony finger at the princess and cried, "In her fifteenth year, the princess shall prick herself with a spindle and fall down dead!"
The court was thrown into terror. But the twelfth Wise Woman stepped forward. Though she could not undo the curse, she could soften it. "The princess shall not die," she said. "She shall fall into a deep sleep lasting one hundred years."
The king ordered every spindle in the kingdom burned. But the prophecy could not be prevented. On her fifteenth birthday, the princess wandered through the castle and discovered a narrow winding staircase she had never seen. Climbing to the top, she found a tiny room where an old woman sat spinning flax. The princess had never seen a spindle. "What is that?" she asked, reaching out. She touched the spindle, pricked her finger, and immediately fell into a deep sleep.
The king, who had just returned to the castle, ordered her placed on a bed of silk and gold. But as he kissed her hand, sleep overcame him too. The queen slept. The courtiers slept. The horses in the stables slept. The dogs in the courtyard slept. The fire on the hearth stopped crackling and fell still. The cook, who had been about to box the kitchen boy's ears, froze mid-motion. The entire castle slept.
A hedge of briar roses grew around the castle walls, thicker each year, until it enclosed everything — towers, gates, walls — in an impenetrable tangle of thorns and blossoms. The kingdom beyond the hedge forgot what lay within, though a legend persisted: a sleeping princess of unearthly beauty waited within the thorns, and she would wake only when a prince of true heart kissed her forehead.
Many princes tried to fight through the briars over the years. The thorns seized them like hands and held them fast until they died tangled and forgotten.
One hundred years passed. A prince from a distant kingdom heard the old wives' tale. His own grandmother told him she had heard from her grandmother about a castle where a princess slept — a princess so beautiful that even the roses around her castle had fallen asleep. The prince laughed at the story, but something drew him to the overgrown path.
When he reached the hedge, the briars were not a wall of thorns but a tunnel of flowers. The roses parted before him like living things that recognized his true heart. He walked through without a scratch.
Inside the castle, everyone slept. He found the princess in her tower chamber, lying on a silk bed, her hands folded over her chest, her face as lovely and peaceful as the day she had fallen asleep. She was more beautiful than any painting or dream.
He knelt beside her. Her beauty was so perfect, so still, that his heart ached. He leaned forward and kissed her forehead.
Her eyes opened. She smiled at him, as if she had only been waiting for this moment. The king woke, the queen woke, the courtiers stretched and yawned as though from an ordinary night's sleep. The horses stamped their hooves, the dogs barked, the fire blazed, and the cook boxed the kitchen boy's ears.
The prince married Briar Rose that very day. The wedding was celebrated with joy throughout the kingdom, and they lived happily all the days of their lives.
moral: True love wakes.

title: The Twelve Brothers
summary: A princess is born after twelve brothers who are cursed to become ravens if she speaks before her twelfth birthday. She must remain silent for twelve years to save them.
full_text:
A king and queen had twelve sons, all strong and healthy. The king, desperate for a daughter, made a terrible vow: if his thirteenth child was a girl, he would have all twelve sons killed so she alone would inherit the kingdom. He even ordered thirteen little coffins to be prepared, filled with wood shavings and laid in a locked room.
The queen discovered the plan. She called her youngest son, Benjamin. "Your father intends to kill you all if I have a daughter. Take your brothers and flee into the forest. I will raise a flag on the castle tower. White means a son has been born and you may return. Red means a daughter — and you must never come back."
The twelve brothers fled to the forest and hid in a cave. After twelve days, a red flag was raised. The brothers were devastated. "Our sister will be our death!" they cried in anger. Benjamin tried to calm them, but they swore revenge on every girl they met. Deep in the forest, they found an enchanted cottage and lived there together, hunting and foraging, their hearts hardened by bitterness.
Meanwhile, the princess grew up not knowing about her brothers. She was beautiful and kind, with a golden star on her forehead. One day, she discovered the thirteen coffins in the locked room and asked her mother about them. The queen, weeping, told her everything.
"I will find them and bring them home," the princess declared. She gathered her belongings and walked into the forest, searching for days. At last, she found the enchanted cottage. Benjamin answered the door. "I am your sister," she said. When the brothers returned, they were furious at first, but seeing her star and her gentleness, their anger melted. They loved her and welcomed her.
The princess stayed with them, keeping house while they hunted. In the garden grew twelve white lilies. She picked them to give her brothers a gift — and at once, the brothers were turned into twelve ravens, their cottage vanished, and the princess was alone.
An old woman appeared. "Foolish child," she said. "Those lilies were your brothers' souls. Now you must make amends. To save them, you must remain completely silent for twelve years — not a word, not a sound. And you must sew twelve shirts from starflowers and nettles."
The princess set to work. She gathered nettles that stung her hands raw and starflowers that bloomed only under moonlight. She sat beneath a tree and wove, speaking not a word.
A king hunting in the forest found her. She was so beautiful that he fell in love and brought her to his castle. Despite her silence, he married her. His mother, the queen dowager, hated the silent bride. "She is a witch," she whispered. "Her silence proves her evil."
The princess gave birth to a son. The queen dowager stole the baby and claimed the princess had killed it. Still, the princess said nothing. She continued weaving her shirts.
A second son was born, and again the queen dowager stole him. Still the princess remained silent. She was put on trial for murder and witchcraft. The older queen produced false witnesses. The king, heartbroken, was forced to sentence her to be burned at the stake.
On the day of her execution, the princess was led to the pyre. She had sewn all twelve shirts except for one, which still lacked a left sleeve. As she walked, twelve ravens descended from the sky. She threw the shirts over them — and they became her twelve brothers, whole and human. The youngest still had a raven's wing instead of a left arm.
"Now I may speak!" she cried, and she told the king everything. The queen dowager was thrown into a pit of vipers for her crimes. The king embraced his wife, and the twelve brothers were restored to their rightful place. The princess had broken the curse — not through words, but through the most powerful language of all: silent, devoted love.
moral: Silent sacrifice and sisterly devotion can break the most powerful curses.

title: The Wolf and the Seven Kids
summary: There was once an old mother goat who lived with her seven little kids in a cozy cottage at the edge of a great forest.
full_text:
There was once an old mother goat who lived with her seven little kids in a cozy cottage at the edge of a great forest. She loved her children dearly and warned them constantly. One morning she needed to gather food, so she called the seven kids around her.
"My dear children," she said, "I must go into the forest. While I am away, be on your guard against the wolf. If he gets inside, he will devour you all, skin, hair, and everything. The wretch often disguises himself, but you will know him by his rough voice and his black paws."
"We will take good care of ourselves," the kids answered bravely. Their mother bleated farewell and trotted into the forest.
It was not long before someone knocked at the door. "Open the door, dear children!" called a rough voice. "Your mother is home with treats for each of you!"
But the kids recognized the wolf's harsh voice. "We will not open the door! You are not our mother. She has a soft, sweet voice. Yours is rough. You are the wolf!"
The wolf ran to a shopkeeper and bought a large piece of chalk. He ate it, softening his voice. He returned and knocked again. "Open the door, dear children! Your mother is home!"
But the wolf had pressed his black paws against the window. The kids saw them. "We will not open the door! Our mother does not have black paws. You are the wolf!"
The wolf ran to a baker. "I have hurt my paw. Spread dough over it." Then he ran to a miller. "Sprinkle white flour on my paw." The miller refused until the wolf threatened to eat him. With his paws now white and his voice soft, the wolf returned a third time.
"Open the door, children," he called sweetly.
"Show us your paws," the kids said. The wolf lifted his white paws to the window. Satisfied, the kids unlatched the door.
The wolf burst in. The kids screamed and scrambled to hide. One dived under the table, another jumped into the bed, a third climbed into the stove, the fourth ran into the kitchen, the fifth squeezed into the cupboard, the sixth crawled under the washbasin, and the smallest kid hid inside the grandfather clock.
But the wolf found them all. One after another, he swallowed them whole — every one except the youngest, hidden in the clock case. Then the wolf, stuffed and satisfied, crept outside and fell asleep under a tree, snoring so loudly that the branches trembled.
When the mother goat returned, she found the cottage in chaos: overturned chairs, scattered dishes, and silence. "My children!" she cried. The youngest kid crept out of the clock case and told her everything. "The wolf ate them all!"
The mother goat's heart broke, but she would not give up. "Show me where he is." The kid led her to the meadow, where the wolf lay snoring beneath an oak tree. His belly was round and twitching.
"Fetch me scissors, a needle, and thread," she whispered. She crept up to the sleeping wolf and carefully cut open his belly. One by one, all six kids tumbled out, alive and unharmed — the wolf had swallowed them whole without chewing.
They hugged and wept with joy. Then the mother goat filled the wolf's belly with heavy stones and sewed him back up. When the wolf woke, he was terribly thirsty. He staggered to the well and leaned over the edge. The stones in his belly pulled him forward, and he fell into the water and drowned.
The seven kids and their mother danced around the well, free from the wolf forever. And from that day on, they never opened the door to anyone without checking both paws and voice — no matter how sweet the tone.
moral: Never open to strangers.

title: The Queen Bee
summary: Two kings' sons once set out on a grand adventure, but they fell into wild and reckless ways and never returned home.
full_text:
Two kings' sons once set out on a grand adventure, but they fell into wild and reckless ways and never returned home. Their younger brother, whom everyone called Simpleton, went to seek them. When he found them at last, they laughed at him, saying he was too simple to make his way in the world. But they continued on together, traveling through strange lands.

They came upon an anthill, and the elder brothers wanted to stir it up for sport, to watch the ants scatter in panic. But Simpleton said, "Leave the little creatures in peace. I will not allow them to be disturbed." The brothers sneered but moved on.

They reached a lake where a flock of ducks swam peacefully. The elder brothers wanted to catch and roast them, but Simpleton said, "Leave the creatures alone. I will not allow you to kill them."

Next, they found a beehive overflowing with honey. The elder brothers wanted to build a fire beneath the tree to suffocate the bees and take the honey. But Simpleton said, "Leave the creatures alone. I will not allow them to be burned."

At last, they came to a great castle, where stone horses stood in the stables and no living soul was to be seen. They explored every hall until they reached a door secured with three heavy locks, and through a small opening, they saw a tiny gray-haired man sitting at a table. They called to him three times before he rose, unlocked the door, and silently led them to a table laden with food and wine. After they ate, he showed them to their chambers.

In the morning, the gray man took the eldest brother to a stone table where three tasks were inscribed. The first task was to gather a thousand pearls hidden in the moss of the forest. If a single pearl was missing by sunset, the seeker would turn to stone. The eldest brother searched all day but found only a hundred. As the sun set, he was turned to stone.

The second brother tried the next day but found only two hundred pearls. He too became stone.

Then Simpleton took his turn. He searched through the moss slowly, finding a pearl here and another there. He grew discouraged, for the task seemed impossible. But the ants he had protected came to his aid. Hundreds of ants scuttled through the moss, finding every pearl and carrying them to a single pile. By sunset, all one thousand pearls were gathered.

The second task was to retrieve the key to the princess's chamber from the bottom of a lake. Simpleton stood at the shore, helpless. The ducks he had saved swam to him, dove into the water, and brought up the key in their beaks.

The third task was to identify the youngest and most beautiful of three sleeping princesses, who looked exactly alike. Simpleton did not know how to choose. But the queen bee he had protected flew to his side and landed on the lips of the youngest princess, for she was the sweetest of all.

Thus, Simpleton broke the castle's enchantment and freed his brothers from their stone prisons. He married the youngest princess and became king, ruling wisely and kindly, and the animals he had befriended always remembered his kindness.
moral: Kindness wins.

title: The Donkey
summary: Once upon a time, in a small German village, there lived a young prince who found himself transformed into a donkey.
full_text:
Once upon a time, in a small German village, there lived a young prince who found himself transformed into a donkey. This was no ordinary affliction but the work of a powerful enchantment cast by a jealous fairy who had been slighted at his christening. The spell was thorough: the prince retained his human mind and gentle heart, but his body was that of a small, long-eared donkey. He could not speak in human tongue, only bray, and he was forced to wander the wilderness, shunned by both humans and animals.

But the prince-turned-donkey did not despair. He discovered that he possessed two remarkable gifts. When he shook his head vigorously, gold coins would tumble from his ears and clatter to the ground. And though his voice was that of a donkey, he had a natural talent for music, particularly the lute. He practiced diligently, sitting by streams in the moonlight, plucking the strings with his clumsy hooves until he could play passably well. The forest creatures gathered to listen, and even the sternest hunters paused to hear his mournful melodies.

One day, a poor miller discovered the donkey grazing in a meadow near his mill. Seeing that the creature was gentle and well-trained, he took it home to help with his work. The miller was a kind but impoverished man, struggling to feed his family. When the donkey shook gold coins from its ears for the first time, the miller wept with joy. He used the gold to buy food for his children, to repair his mill, and to build a better life.

Word of the miller's sudden wealth spread through the village. The miller's wife, a greedy woman who had always wanted more than they had, demanded to know the source of their fortune. When she discovered the donkey's secret, she insisted they charge travelers to see the miraculous animal. The donkey grew weary of being stared at and discussed like a curiosity, but he endured it for the miller's sake.

One day, the miller's son, a kind young man who had always treated the donkey with affection, discovered the truth: the donkey was actually a prince. The son vowed to help break the spell. He learned that only true love's kiss from a princess could undo the enchantment. So the son took the donkey and set out for the royal palace, where a beautiful princess lived.

The son played the lute while the donkey danced, and their performance charmed the princess. She laughed at the donkey's antics and was moved by his soulful eyes. When the son explained the donkey's plight, she agreed to help. She kissed the donkey on his furry cheek, and in a flash of golden light, the donkey transformed into a handsome prince.

The prince thanked the miller's son and the princess, and he asked the princess to marry him. She accepted, and they were wed in a grand ceremony. The prince invited the miller and his family to live in the palace, and they never wanted for anything again. The miller's wife learned to be content with what she had, and the prince ruled with wisdom and kindness, remembering his days as a donkey and never forgetting the value of humility and gratitude.
moral: Love sees past form.

title: The Nutcracker and the Mouse King
summary: The Monkey King, Sun Wukong, is the greatest trickster in all of Chinese mythology.
full_text:
The Monkey King, Sun Wukong, is the greatest trickster in all of Chinese mythology. He was born from a stone egg on the Mountain of Flowers and Fruit, fertilized by the wind and the rain. He was no ordinary monkey. He immediately impressed the other monkeys by leaping through a waterfall and discovering a hidden cave. They made him their king.

But Sun Wukong was not content to rule monkeys. He wanted immortality. He traveled across China to find a Taoist master who taught him the secrets of the gods. He learned 72 transformations — he could become a bird, a tree, a grain of rice, a temple. He learned cloud-somersaulting, which carried him 54,000 miles in a single leap. He acquired a magical staff, the Ruyi Jingu Bang, which could shrink to the size of a needle or grow to the thickness of a pillar.

Armed with his powers, Sun Wukong stormed Heaven itself. He defeated the Four Heavenly Kings, the 28 Constellations, and the entire army of Heaven. He ate the Peaches of Immortality, drank the Elixir of Life, and declared himself the Great Sage, Equal of Heaven. The Jade Emperor, ruler of Heaven, was forced to beg the Buddha for help.

The Buddha challenged Sun Wukong to prove his power. "Can you jump out of my hand?" the Buddha asked. Sun Wukong laughed and leaped across the universe, finally landing at the base of five pink pillars. He marked one with his urine and leaped back. "I have crossed the universe," he boasted. But the Buddha laughed. "You never left my palm. The five pillars were my fingers."

Sun Wukong was trapped under a mountain for 500 years. Eventually, he was released to serve the monk Xuanzang on the Journey to the West, protecting him from demons in exchange for his freedom. The Monkey King learned humility through suffering. He discovered that true strength is not the power to take what you want, but the wisdom to serve something greater than yourself.
moral: Sisters save brothers; love conquers all curses.

title: The Elves and the Shoemaker
summary: On the misty moors of Devon and Cornwall, where the fog rolls in from the sea and covers the land like a blanket, there is a light that leads travelers to their doom.
full_text:
On the misty moors of Devon and Cornwall, where the fog rolls in from the sea and covers the land like a blanket, there is a light that leads travelers to their doom. The will-o'-the-wisp has many names — jack-o'-lantern, corpse candle, friar's lantern — but its nature is always the same: it is a false hope that ends in tragedy.

The will-o'-the-wisp appears as a flickering blue or green light, bobbing and weaving over marshy ground. It looks like a lantern carried by someone just ahead. A traveler lost in the fog will see the light and think it is a friendly cottage or a village. They will follow it. The light always leads them deeper into the marsh, where the ground turns to quicksand and the reeds grow thick as prison bars.

The scientific explanation is simple: marsh gas — methane released by decaying vegetation — ignites spontaneously in the air. But the villagers of old England knew better. They said the will-o'-the-wisp is the soul of a wicked person denied entry to both heaven and hell, doomed to wander the earth with a single coal of hellfire to light his way.

There is a story from Dartmoor of a man named Will who was known for his dishonesty. He cheated his neighbors, broke his promises, and laughed at the church. When he died, the devil took his soul but found it too corrupt even for hell. The devil forged Will's soul into a lantern and tossed it back to earth. "Go," the devil said. "Lead the living into the marshes, and you will have company in your misery."

Will-o'-the-wisps can be defeated by turning your coat inside out and carrying it over your arm. This confuses the spirit, making it think you are someone else. But the safest strategy is to never follow strange lights in the fog. If you are lost on the moor, sit down, light a lantern of your own, and wait for daylight. The will-o'-the-wisp can only deceive those who are desperate enough to follow.
moral: Help is always given; gratitude returns blessings.

title: The Fur Coat
summary: In a cold German winter, in a village nestled deep in the Black Forest, a poor girl was walking home through a forest when she found a fur coat lying in the snow.
full_text:
In a cold German winter, in a village nestled deep in the Black Forest, a poor girl was walking home through a forest when she found a fur coat lying in the snow.
